input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a cyber-physical system (CPS) inspired by harbor cranes used for moving small pucks (representing containers) between predefined locations. The physical twin (PT) consists of the **gantry crane hardware** (including frame, motors, controllers, electromagnet, and sensors) and its **embedded controller** (Arduino-based system for measurement and control). The crane operates in a controlled lab environment with a **Bill-of-Materials (BOM) costing approximately €1250**, primarily driven by Trinamic motor drivers and an Arduino-based measurement system. The PT is designed for **real-time operation** with bidirectional interaction between physical and digital components, enabling research in digital twin validation, fault detection, and system evolution.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical acting components of the system include:
  - **Two stepper motors** (cart and hoist) controlled via **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers** for precise movement.
  - **Electromagnet** at the hoist end for picking up pucks/containers.
  - **Arduino-based control system** integrating sensors and actuators via a custom KiCad-designed shield for simplified connectivity.
  The acting components are directly interfaced with the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python-based) to enable virtual-to-physical control commands.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The physical sensing components include:
  - **Swing Angle Encoder** to measure the swinging motion of the hoist.
  - **Anemometer** to measure wind speed (potentially affecting crane dynamics).
  - **Arduino-based sensor integration** for real-time data acquisition of motor positions, electromagnet state, and environmental factors (e.g., wind). Data is transmitted to the digital twin via **MQTT protocol** over a wired/wireless connection to the **MQTT Broker** (Eclipse Mosquitto).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The physical-to-virtual interaction involves **real-time data streaming** from the PT to the digital twin (DT) via the following mechanisms:
  - **Continuous logging** of crane state (e.g., motor positions, swing angles, electromagnet status) to a **TimescaleDB** database via the **Logging Application** (Python-based).
  - **Event-driven data transmission** (e.g., trajectory completion, fault detection) triggered by sensor inputs or controller events. Data is published to the **MQTT Broker** (Eclipse Mosquitto) for distribution to DT services (e.g., Continuous Validation Service, Dashboard).
  - **Historical data logging** with timestamps for post-processing and validation. The interaction rate is **real-time** (millisecond-scale for critical parameters like motor positions).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The virtual-to-physical interaction is facilitated through **control commands and optimized trajectories** generated by the DT and executed on the PT:
  - **Trajectory Generation Service** computes optimal movement paths (e.g., swing-free trajectories) using a **kinematic model** of the crane, which are sent to the **Control Application** (Python) via MQTT.
  - **Simulation Service** validates trajectories before execution, simulating crane behavior under identical conditions.
  - **Continuous Validation Service** compares simulated vs. executed trajectories and alerts operators via the **Dashboard** (Grafana) if deviations exceed thresholds. Commands are transmitted to the PT via the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python) and executed by the **Arduino-based controller**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The digital twin provides the following services:
  - **Optimal Trajectory Generation**: Uses a **kinematic model** to compute swing-free trajectories for efficient crane operation.
  - **Continuous Validation**: Compares executed trajectories (from PT) with simulated trajectories (from Simulation Service) using **distance metrics** and thresholds; alerts operators via Grafana dashboard if discrepancies exceed limits.
  - **Data Logging**: Persists crane state data (e.g., motor positions, swing angles) in **TimescaleDB** for historical analysis.
  - **Data Visualization**: Real-time and historical data visualization via **Grafana dashboard**, including trajectory traces with confidence bounds from simulations.
  - **Simulation**: A **crane simulation service** replicates PT behavior for offline validation and testing of control strategies.
  - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**: A Python-based frontend for operators to configure crane settings, monitor status, and log configurations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The digital twin operates on **real-time and near-real-time scales**:
  - **Physical-to-virtual interaction**: Data is logged and transmitted at **millisecond-to-second intervals** (e.g., motor positions, swing angles) for continuous validation.
  - **Virtual-to-physical interaction**: Control commands and trajectories are generated and executed with **low-latency** (sub-second) to ensure synchronization with PT dynamics.
  - **Validation and simulation**: Continuous validation runs at **real-time or slightly delayed** intervals (e.g., post-trajectory completion) to compare executed vs. simulated paths. The system supports **event-driven synchronization** (e.g., trajectory completion triggers validation).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The digital twin system is **centralized** with a single, unified digital representation of the PT, though it includes **multiple internal twins** for different purposes:
  - **Physical Twin (PT)**: The actual gantry crane hardware and controller.
  - **Simulation Twin**: A software-based replica of the PT used for offline validation and trajectory testing.
  - **Validation Twin**: A service that continuously compares PT data with simulation outputs to detect deviations.
  - **Data Twin**: The **TimescaleDB** database storing historical and real-time PT data.
  - **Visualization Twin**: The **Grafana dashboard** for operator interaction and monitoring.
  The system does not employ **decentralized twins** (e.g., edge computing or federated twins) but relies on a **centralized MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto)** for inter-service communication.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The digital twin covers the **as-operated** lifecycle stage of the physical system, focusing on **runtime monitoring, validation, and optimization** during operation. It does not explicitly model the **as-designed** (ideal) or **as-manufactured** stages, though CAD models (Autodesk Fusion 360) and kinematic models are used for trajectory planning and simulation. The DT is primarily deployed for **operational phase** activities such as fault detection, continuous validation, and performance optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The digital twin comprises the following models and data components:
  - **CAD Models**: 3D models of the crane (Autodesk Fusion 360) used for visualization and kinematic analysis.
  - **Kinematic Model**: Mathematical representation of crane dynamics for trajectory generation and simulation.
  - **Simulation Model**: Software-based replica of the PT implemented in the **Simulation Service** for offline validation.
  - **Continuous Validation Model**: A **distance metric-based comparator** that evaluates deviations between executed and simulated trajectories.
  - **Data Models**: Structured data schemas in **TimescaleDB** for logging crane state (e.g., motor positions, swing angles, electromagnet status) with timestamps. Data is organized as time-series for efficient querying.
  - **Arcadia Models**: Capella-based models (for Model-Based Systems Engineering) used in teaching contexts (e.g., SpaceRaise doctoral school).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The digital twin leverages the following tools and enablers:
  - **Software**:
    - **Python** (for all DT services: Logging, Control, Trajectory Generation, Simulation, Validation).
    - **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL-based time-series database for data persistence).
    - **Grafana** (for real-time and historical data visualization).
    - **Eclipse Mosquitto** (MQTT broker for inter-service communication).
    - **Docker** (containerization for database, MQTT broker, and dashboard).
  - **Hardware**:
    - **Arduino** (for sensor integration and control).
    - **TMC4671-Eval** motor controllers (for stepper motor actuation).
    - **Custom KiCad shield** for Arduino sensor connectivity.
  - **Development Frameworks**:
    - **ROS-inspired ad-hoc approach** (no formal ROS middleware, but Python-based modular design).
    - **GitHub repository** with **Docker Compose** for easy deployment.
  - **Modeling Tools**:
    - **Autodesk Fusion 360** (CAD modeling).
    - **Capella (Arcadia)** (Model-Based Systems Engineering for teaching).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The digital twin constellation is organized as a **modular, service-oriented architecture** with the following components:
  - **Physical Layer**: Gantry crane hardware (motors, controllers, sensors) and Arduino-based interface.
  - **Digital Twin Layer**:
    - **Data Layer**: TimescaleDB (database) and MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto).
    - **Service Layer**:
      - **Trajectory Generator Service** (kinematic modeling).
      - **Simulation Service** (PT replica for offline validation).
      - **Continuous Validation Service** (real-time comparison of executed vs. simulated trajectories).
      - **Logging Application** (data ingestion from PT).
    - **Visualization Layer**: Grafana dashboard for operator interaction.
  - **Interface Layer**: Python-based **Physical Crane Interface** and **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** for operator control.
  The constellation follows a **publish-subscribe model** (MQTT) for inter-service communication and **REST-like interactions** for data querying (TimescaleDB).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process involves the following stages and evolution mechanisms:
  - **Development Process**:
    - **Requirements**: Focused on real-time validation, fault detection, and trajectory optimization.
    - **Design**: Modular Python services with clear separation of concerns (e.g., logging, control, validation).
    - **Implementation**: Python-based services integrated via MQTT; Docker for containerized deployment.
    - **Validation**: Continuous validation of trajectories using simulation comparisons and time-series classification for fault detection.
  - **Evolution Milestones**:
    - **Initial Version**: Basic logging, visualization, and trajectory generation (used in [4]).
    - **Continuous Validation**: Integration of model validation techniques ([5]).
    - **Fault Detection**: Extension with time-series classification ([6]).
    - **DarTwin Notation**: Formalization of digital twin evolution ([7]).
    - **Teaching Extensions**: Expansion to harbor systems and Capella models for educational use.
  - **Quality Assurance**: Validation metrics (distance thresholds) and operator alerts for deviations. The process is **iterative**, with evolutions derived from research publications and teaching needs.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The digital twin employs the following fidelity and validity mechanisms:
  - **Model Fidelity**:
    - **Kinematic Model**: High fidelity for trajectory generation, validated against PT behavior.
    - **Simulation Model**: Replicates PT dynamics with **confidence bounds** (upper/lower limits) for trajectory validation.
  - **Validation Methods**:
    - **Continuous Validation**: Compares executed trajectories (from PT) with simulated trajectories using **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean distance between paths). Thresholds are set to detect anomalies.
    - **Fault Detection**: Uses **time-series classification techniques** (e.g., [6]) to identify deviations from expected behavior.
    - **Uncertainty Handling**: Simulation confidence bounds provide a range for expected trajectory deviations; alerts are triggered if PT data exceeds these bounds.
  - **Verification**:
    - **Unit Testing**: Python-based tests for individual services (e.g., trajectory generation, validation logic).
    - **Integration Testing**: Validation of end-to-end workflows (e.g., trajectory execution → simulation comparison).
  - **Uncertainty Sources**: Environmental factors (e.g., wind speed) and sensor noise are not explicitly modeled but are mitigated via continuous validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The technical connection between the physical twin (PT) and digital twin (DT) is established via:
  - **Network Protocol**: **MQTT (Eclipse Mosquitto)** for real-time, lightweight messaging between services and PT sensors/controllers.
  - **Data Transmission**:
    - **Physical-to-Virtual**: Sensor data (e.g., motor positions, swing angles) is streamed to the **MQTT Broker** via Arduino-Python interface.
    - **Virtual-to-Physical**: Control commands (e.g., trajectory commands) are published to the **MQTT Broker** and subscribed by the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python).
  - **Latency**: Designed for **low-latency** (<100ms) to ensure synchronization between PT and DT.
  - **Topology**: **Star topology** with the MQTT Broker as the central hub for all communications. The PT connects to the DT via a **wired/wireless network** (exact protocol not specified but assumed to be TCP/IP-based).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The digital twin is hosted and deployed on a **personal computer** with the following technical setup:
  - **Hardware**: Single PC running all DT services (Python-based) and containerized components (Docker).
  - **Containerization**: **Docker Compose** manages the deployment of:
    - **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL-based time-series database).
    - **Eclipse Mosquitto** (MQTT broker).
    - **Grafana** (dashboard).
  - **Software Stack**:
    - **Python 3.x** (for all services: logging, control, trajectory generation, validation).
    - **Docker Engine** (for container orchestration).
    - **GitHub Repository** (hosts the entire DT system, including CAD models, Arduino code, and documentation).
  - **Deployment Workflow**:
    - **First-time setup**: Docker Compose automates database initialization, dashboard configuration, and MQTT broker setup.
    - **Runtime**: All services run as separate processes (Python) or containers (Docker), with MQTT enabling inter-service communication. The PT connects directly to the PC via USB/ethernet for sensor/actuator data.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The digital twin provides the following insights and decision-making outputs (indirectly affecting the PT):
  - **Trajectory Optimization Insights**: Identifies optimal paths (e.g., swing-free trajectories) to improve crane efficiency and reduce energy consumption.
  - **Fault Detection Insights**: Uses time-series classification to pinpoint anomalies (e.g., motor misalignment, sensor failures) without direct PT intervention.
  - **Validation Metrics**: Distance metrics between executed and simulated trajectories highlight deviations from expected behavior, enabling proactive maintenance.
  - **Operational Alerts**: Grafana dashboard notifies operators of threshold breaches (e.g., trajectory errors, sensor failures), allowing manual or automated corrective actions.
  - **Research Insights**: Enables exploration of digital twin evolution (e.g., DarTwin notation) and continuous validation methods for cyber-physical systems.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The digital twin exhibits **limited horizontal integration** with external systems beyond its immediate constellation:
  - **No Explicit Integration**: The DT does not connect to external information systems (e.g., ERP, MES, or other DTs) as part of its core functionality.
  - **Teaching Extensions**: In educational contexts, the DT was expanded to include a **harbor system** (e.g., container identification, ship buoyancy models) for broader system-of-systems demonstrations, but this is not part of the core exemplar.
  - **Data Sharing**: Historical data in TimescaleDB could theoretically be integrated with external tools (e.g., analytics platforms) via SQL interfaces, but this is not implemented in the current setup.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
The data ownership and privacy aspects are as follows:
  - **Data Ownership**: The digital twin system is **open-source (MIT license)**, and the data generated by the PT and DT is owned by the **researcher/educator deploying the system**. The PT owner (e.g., lab/university) retains control over the physical hardware and associated data.
  - **Privacy Considerations**:
    - No explicit **GDPR compliance** mechanisms are documented, as the system is designed for **controlled lab environments** with limited sensitive data.
    - Data is stored locally (TimescaleDB on the hosting PC) and not transmitted to external servers unless explicitly configured.
    - **No user authentication** is implemented for the Grafana dashboard or MQTT broker, though this could be added for security in production environments.
  - **Access Control**: Access to the DT system is restricted to authorized users (e.g., researchers, operators) via local network access to the hosting PC.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The digital twin adheres to the following standards and conventions:
  - **Modeling Standards**:
    - **AutomationML** (mentioned in related work but not used in this exemplar).
    - **Capella (Arcadia)** for Model-Based Systems Engineering (used in teaching contexts).
  - **Data Standards**:
    - **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL-based) for time-series data storage, leveraging SQL for querying.
    - **MQTT v3.1.1** (Eclipse Mosquitto) for inter-service communication.
  - **Software Standards**:
    - **Python 3.x** (widely adopted for scripting and automation).
    - **Docker** (containerization standard for deployment).
    - **MIT License** for open-source distribution.
  - **No Industry-Specific Frameworks**: The exemplar does not use commercial digital twin frameworks (e.g., Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx) but follows an **ad-hoc, modular approach** with open-source tools.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the digital twin include:
  - **Security**:
    - **No Remote Execution**: The DT does not remotely execute operations on the PT; all commands are validated locally before execution.
    - **Local Network Access**: The PT and DT communicate over a **local network** (PC-based), reducing exposure to external threats.
    - **No Encryption**: Data transmission between PT and DT is not encrypted (assumed to be in a **trusted lab environment**).
    - **Access Control**: Limited to local users; no explicit authentication for MQTT or Grafana (potential risk in shared environments).
  - **Safety**:
    - **Manual Override**: Operators can manually intervene via the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** to halt or adjust crane operations.
    - **Threshold-Based Alerts**: Continuous validation triggers alerts for trajectory deviations, allowing operators to take corrective action.
    - **No Autonomous Hazard Mitigation**: The DT does not autonomously shut down the PT in case of critical failures; alerts are for informational purposes only.
  - **Human Safety**: The PT operates in a **controlled lab environment** with no direct human interaction risks (e.g., no heavy lifting or hazardous materials). Safety is ensured via manual oversight and basic fault detection.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar Joost Mertens _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be _**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a** **large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly** **available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and** **reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-** **scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises** **both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical** **side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital** **side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,** **and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,** **data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup** **as use case in several previous publications where its functionality** **was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other** **freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope** **it can be used for future research or education on the topic of** **digital twins.** _**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-** **physical-system** I. I NTRODUCTION The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual systems and services derived therefrom. During operation data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3]. Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating research results requires relevant use cases. Such research results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a notation for system evolution [7]. In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in various domains would help reduce development overhead, and additionally also also help support the generalizability of research results. Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education is another field for which well described exemplars can be Joachim Denil _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joachim.denil@uantwerpen.be Fig. 1. The physical gantry crane. beneficial, aiding teachers with explaining and demonstrating the digital twin concept, and training engineers with the necessary experience to set up such systems in the real world. In this paper, we present an openly available digital twin exemplar of a lab-scale gantry crane. It is inspired by cranes in the harbor of Antwerp, which move containers between moored ships and the quay. It is a typical Cyber-Physical System and consists of: _•_ The physical gantry crane including its controller. _•_ The crane’s digital twin containing CAD models, services for data logging, data visualization, optimal trajectory generation, and continuous validation. The exemplar is hosted on Cosys-Lab’s organizational GitHub [1] . A picture of the physical crane is shown in fig. 1. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: section II first discusses related exemplars from literature. Section III discusses our exemplar in detail, both architecturally and implementation wise. Afterwards section IV discusses the results and the publications that feature this exemplar. Next, section V discusses some limitations and future extensions. Finally section VI concludes the paper. 1 [https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane](https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane) II. B ACKGROUND & R ELATED W ORK A search with the query “digital twin exemplar” on Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar and GitHub’s search function yielded other openly available digital twin exemplars with accompanying publications. In what follows, we briefly discuss the most noteworthy ones. _A. ARCHES PiCar-X_ The PiCar-X is a little Raspberry-Pi based robotic car that is used by Barbie and Hasselbring as a demonstrator of various digital twin concepts [8], [9]. These digital twin concepts [10] were originally used in the ARCHES project on autonomous robotic networks [11], but the authors wanted to make these results reproducible in a lab-scale setting with an affordable and straightforward example. The exemplar is hosted on GitHub [2] and contains documentation on how to get the exemplar up and running. Emulators are also provided such that the physical system is not necessarily needed. The exemplar features technologies such as Docker containerization to encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES Digital Twin Framework which is built on top of the ROS (Robot Operation System) middleware. _B. GreenhouseDT_ GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse [12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with prerecorded data. _C. Air Quality Management_ The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14]. Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented digital twin pipeline. _D. Discussion_ The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses 2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X) 3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT) 4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar) Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system. a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are more ad-hoc. In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework. The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars. III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves small pucks which represents containers from one location to another. In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system with a context and container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system. _A. Architecture_ Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital twin research on it. Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to keep the diagram readable. The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital twin if needed, consists of five containers. **Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane. 5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/) 6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for something that must be there for the system to work. Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system. **Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane. **Control Application:** a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane. **Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database. **Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane. The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe them bottom up. **Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system and its various services. **Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from the database. **Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no residual swinging motion at the end. **Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator **Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action. **MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between services. Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give an overview of the components powering it. We should note that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram. Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity and readability only the most necessary relationships are shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane. Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An **Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers, a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An **Arduino** measures and controls those last three components. Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane. _B. Implementation_ Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution of the C4 Containers visually. For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital twin services. For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and **Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically handled by this docker compose file. Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database, the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i) it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source. Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used. _C. Repository Organization_ Listing 1 shows the repository organization. The **BOM** folder contains the Bill-of-Materials. The **C4-diagrams** folder contains the diagrams used in this paper. The **CAD** folder contains CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fu sion360. The **arcadia** folder contains Arcadia models made in Capella for a lecture on Model-Based Systems Engineering that starred the crane. The **arduino** folder contains the code to deploy on the measurement and control Arduino, as well as hardware schematics and a board for a custom shield made in KiCad that simplifies connectivity of the sensor to the Arduino. The **docker** folder contains the docker compose file and all associated first-time setup files for the dashboard, database and MQTT broker. The **docs** folder contains the documentation made in MkDocs and hosted on GitHub Pages. The **examples** folder contains basic examples of the gantrylib module. The **gantrylib** folder then contains the module itself. The **test** folder contains test associated with the gantrylib module. The **LICENSE** file contains the MIT license under which the exemplar is published. The **README** gives a brief overview of the repository and redirects to github pages for the installation. Lastly, **mkdocs.yml** contains some MkDocs configuration and **setup.py** contains the metadata of the gantrylib module. **Listing 1** Repository organization. ./ |-- BOM/ |-- C4-diagrams |-- CAD/ |-- arcadia/ |-- arduino/ |-- docker/ |-- docs/ |-- examples/ |-- gantrylib/ |-- tests/ |-- LICENSE |-- README.md |-- mkdocs.yml |-- setup.py IV. In it are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing, the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester. At the Gran Sasso Science Institute (GSSI) the exemplar was used during the SpaceRaise doctoral school to teach about digital twinning in practice [7] . On this first day talks were given on the topic of twinning in Systems Engineering, and the exemplar was used to practically summarize the topics presented that day. Specifically for this doctoral school, the Capella models were developed. V. D ISCUSSION AND F UTURE W ORK The gantry crane has proven it’s merit for both research and education, but nonetheless it has some limitations or shortcomings which we discuss here. _A. Cost_ Firstly, with the current BOM the crane is not low cost, at approximately 1250 Euro for one crane in parts. Almost half of this cost stems from the Trinamic Motor Drivers, for which a development kit is used. The logical next step is to forego the development kits and the measurement Arduino 7 [https://spaceraise.academy/#courses Week 2, May 19th 2025](https://spaceraise.academy/#courses) with its shield, and develop a custom driver board integrating these three microcontrollers. This would greatly bring the cost down. _B. Size_ The current size of the crane was chosen based on the dimensions of the location in the lab. This size, however, is larger than the common size of checked luggage accepted by airlines, making it cumbersome to transport. _C. Teaching Extensions_ During teaching we made extensions to the gantry crane: for the BIP more of the harbor was included, and for the doctoral school we focused on Capella models. We deemed those extensions a little too immature to include as features in this paper, in the future they must be further developed, especially the ship and harbor simulations for the BIP. _D. Lack of Physical Simulation_ Currently, there is no physical crane simulator. To make the exemplar even more accessible, a completely simulated version of the physical crane could be included, as was done in the exemplars mentioned in the related work. This researchers or educators can test the project without committing to building the rather costly crane. This could also open up uses on the topic of virtual commissioning. _E. Digital Twin Frameworks_ Over the past years a multitude of digital twin frameworks have been developed, many of them open-source [15], [16]. Examples are Eclipse Ditto, for Internet-of-Things devices and their digital twins, or Eclipse BaSyx for Asset Administration Shell driven digital twins. Our approach is much more ad-hoc, and more in line with the approach taken in [17]. We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins. One could argue for or against this, in our opinion the ad-hoc approach allows more freedom and less overhead, but usage of the frameworks would surely make the exemplar more industry relevant. VI. C ONCLUSION We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed to support digital twin research and education. It expands the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case, reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility was illustrated with examples from our personal academic publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar, it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which we provided pointers for future work.
